summary: Kenneth demonstrates a covered call strategy on HIMS, generating $450 in premium by selling Friday expiration calls at the 32 strike (near $31 stock price) on 300 flexible shares. He uses technical analysis (RSI, volume, chart patterns) to identify profit-taking opportunities after a 67% rally, accepting assignment risk in exchange for maximum premium collection.
takeaways: Sell covered calls at strike prices just out-of-the-money (1-3% above current price) to maximize premium while accepting higher assignment probability
Use technical indicators (RSI, volume) and chart patterns to identify profit-taking zones after strong rallies as optimal entry points for selling calls
Recognize that selling covered calls on volatile stocks creates two competing risks: upside cap (if stock rallies hard) and share loss (if assigned)
Weekly expirations (Friday expiration dates) allow faster premium decay and frequent re-positioning opportunities
Maintain a two-tier position structure: core shares (long-term holdings) vs. flexible shares (tactical call-selling candidates)
If unwilling to lose shares or cap upside, simply do not sell covered calls—the strategy fundamentally requires accepting these outcomes
Further out-of-the-money strikes (e.g., 34 vs. 32) reduce assignment likelihood but collect less premium, requiring a conscious trade-off decision

strategy_candidates.change: Implement a systematic technical analysis filter (RSI >70 + recent >40% rally) before selling covered calls on flexible position tier to improve premium quality and assignment consistency
strategy_candidates.evidence: Kenneth specifically identified HIMS 67% rally + RSI conditions as the trigger to sell calls; applying this repeatable framework across the $50k portfolio's covered call rotation could increase hit rate and monthly $1,250 premium target reliability

title: $450 on HIMS covered calls
